blocks.content: She was smiling. She was aware that she was smiling, alone in her room, holding her phone in both hands like something she didn't want to drop.

She typed and deleted a few things. None of them were quite right. She wanted to say: I love you. She wanted to say: I've been wanting to tell you something for months and I've been afraid and tonight something in me stopped being afraid. She wanted to say: you look at me like I'm the right answer to something you didn't know you were asking, and I have spent years not being looked at like that, and I don't know what to do with it except try to deserve it.
She said none of those things.
I'll text you in the morning.

She put the phone down on the nightstand. She lay back on the bed. The small lamp was still on — that warm, soft light that made things look the way they look in the good moments of old photographs. She looked at the ceiling.
